To determine which set of odd natural numbers $ n < 10000 $ is larger — those for which the number formed by the last four digits of $ n^9 $ is greater than $ n $, or those for which it is less than $ n $ — we must consider the behavior of modular arithmetic and the properties of powers of odd numbers.

Odd numbers raised to any power remain odd, so $ n^9 $ will always be odd. However, the last four digits of $ n^9 $ depend on the value of $ n \mod 10000 $, and since $ n < 10000 $, $ n \mod 10000 = n $. Therefore, the last four digits of $ n^9 $ are simply the last four digits of $ n^9 $, which is equivalent to $ n^9 \mod 10000 $.

The key insight is that for many values of $ n $, $ n^9 \mod 10000 $ will be significantly larger than $ n $, especially as $ n $ increases. For smaller values of $ n $, $ n^9 \mod 10000 $ may be smaller than $ n $, but as $ n $ grows, the magnitude of $ n^9 $ increases rapidly, making it more likely that $ n^9 \mod 10000 $ is greater than $ n $.

However, since $ n < 10000 $, the values of $ n $ are relatively small, and the number formed by the last four digits of $ n^9 $ can be less than $ n $, equal to $ n $, or greater than $ n $, depending on the specific value of $ n $. 

By analyzing the distribution of $ n^9 \mod 10000 $ for all odd $ n < 10000 $, it can be shown that the number of odd natural numbers $ n < 10000 $ for which the last four digits of $ n^9 $ are greater than $ n $ is larger than the number for which they are less than $ n $.
